questions.question: Explain the Master Theorem and its three cases for analyzing divide-and-conquer recurrences.
keyPoints: Case 1: f(n) < n^(log_b a) → solution n^(log_b a)
Case 2: f(n) = n^(log_b a) → solution n^(log_b a) log n
Case 3: f(n) > n^(log_b a) with regularity → solution f(n)

questions.question: Solve the recurrence T(n)=2T(n/2)+n using recursion tree method.
keyPoints: Draw recursion tree with log n levels
Sum of n work over log n levels = n log n

questions.question: What is the substitution method for solving recurrences? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Define substitution method as guess and verify
Steps: guess form, substitute, prove by induction
Example: T(n)=2T(n/2)+n, guess T(n)≤cn log n
Show induction works for suitable c

questions.question: Analyze the time and space complexity of recursive binary search.
keyPoints: Time: T(n)=O(log n) using Master Theorem
Space: O(log n) for recursion stack

questions.question: Explain iteration method for solving recurrences with example T(n)=T(n-1)+n.
What is the recurrence for QuickSort? Analyze its best and worst case complexity.
keyPoints: Best case: balanced partition T(n)=2T(n/2)+O(n) → O(n log n)

questions.question: Differentiate between homogeneous and non-homogeneous recurrences with examples.
keyPoints: Homogeneous: only T terms, no extra function (e.g., T(n)=2T(n/2))
Non-homogeneous: has additional f(n) term (e.g., T(n)=2T(n/2)+n)
Explain how this affects solution methods

questions.question: Using Master Theorem, analyze the time complexity of: (a) T(n)=3T(n/2)+n² (b) T(n)=4T(n/3)+n (c) T(n)=2T(n/4)+1
keyPoints: Apply conditions and verify each case

questions.question: Solve T(n)=T(n-1)+T(n-2)+O(1) using recursion tree and explain why naive Fibonacci is inefficient. Suggest improvements.
keyPoints: Draw recursion tree showing exponential growth
Internal nodes also O(2^n), total O(2^n)
Solution: dynamic programming - O(n) time and space
Or memoization for O(n) time with caching

questions.question: Analyze Tower of Hanoi algorithm: T(n)=2T(n-1)+O(1). Solve the recurrence and explain its implication for practical use.
Compare and analyze: Binary Search vs Linear Search using recurrence relations. Also compare their space complexities.
keyPoints: Binary search: T(n)=T(n/2)+O(1), Solution: O(log n)
Space: Binary search O(log n), Linear search O(1) iterative / O(n) recursive
Conclusion: Binary search is exponentially faster for sorted data
